title: 🐞 Common Bug: Closure Captures a Variable from the Outer Scope
subtitle: Avoid closure bugs in Go goroutines by passing loop or shared variables as function parameters to ensure safe, race-free execution.
excerpt: Avoid closure bugs in Go goroutines by passing loop or shared variables as function parameters to ensure safe, race-free execution.

content.text: 🐞 Common Bug: Closure Captures a Variable from the Outer Scope

content.html: A closure is a function that captures variables from the surrounding scope. In Go, when you define an anonymous function (like in a goroutine) that uses a variable from outside, it’s not copying the variable — it’s referencing it directly.
Using the goroutine function without sending a variable as a parameter could cause bugs in the application.

content.alt: 🐞 Common Bug: Closure Captures a Variable from the Outer Scope

content.html: To fix the “book” Variables in your goroutine, they must be correctly captured and not shadowed or unexpectedly shared due to closures.

items: The “book” is being accessed inside the goroutine, but it was likely declared outside and may change before the goroutine runs (due to how closures work in Go).
This can cause a race condition or unexpected “book” values inside the goroutine.

content.alt: 🐞 Common Bug: Closure Captures a Variable from the Outer Scope

content.text: ✅ Correct Approach: Capture the Variable Properly

content.html: Instead of relying on the closure capturing the variable, you can pass it explicitly as a parameter:

content.code: go func(ct context.Context, book Book) {    t.sample(ct, book)}(ctx, *book)

items: Ensures the current value “book” is preserved.
Now the goroutine gets its copy of the book value at the time the goroutine is created.
The goroutine function takes a “book” parameter, making it safe and deterministic.
This eliminates the closure over the book variable from the outer scope.
This pattern is safe, deterministic, and avoids the potential race conditions or incorrect values.
